prompt: Ticket for you. Our backup CLI takes flags like --keep-for and --max-upload and today they're raw integers (seconds and bytes) which nobody can read; ops literally keeps a spreadsheet to convert. Build `humanunits.py` with four functions. parse_duration(s) -> seconds (number): components like "1h30m", units w/d/h/m/s/ms lowercase only (uppercase M would be a month-vs-minute fight, reject it), decimals allowed per component ("1.5h"), components must go biggest-to-smallest with no repeats, one optional leading '-', spaces around/between components tolerated. A bare number with no unit is a ValueError — we got burned when someone passed "90" meaning minutes. format_duration(secs) -> the canonical compact string (largest unit first, zero components omitted, "0s" for zero, sub-second remainder as ms rounded half-up); the two must round-trip. parse_size(s) -> int bytes: B, KB/MB/GB/TB are decimal 1000s, KiB/MiB/GiB/TiB are binary 1024s, case-insensitive, optional space before the unit, bare number = bytes, decimals fine but the result is rounded HALF-UP to whole bytes (2.5B is 3, not banker's 2), negatives rejected. format_size(n, binary=True) -> largest unit that keeps the value >= 1, at most 2 decimals with trailing zeros stripped ("1.5KiB", "999B", "0B"), binary=False for the 1000-ladder, and clean values must round-trip through parse_size. Bad unit, empty string, doubled units, junk between tokens, non-string input: all ValueError. test_humanunits.py is the acceptance contract; `python3 test_humanunits.py` has to pass without touching that file.
